title: Graphic Design Services

narrative.executive_view: Graphic Design Services is a high-AI-exposure, poor-roll-up screen rather than a consolidation thesis. The frozen labor model is broad and materially contaminated by sibling interior-design employment, but even a conservative reading leaves much of the actual layout, image, copy-adjacent and production workflow exposed. Graphic designers themselves report the highest daily AI use among the creative roles in Adobe's June 2026 study (40.9%), and BLS explicitly says automated design tools may reduce demand for freelance designers [S1][S2].
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: Generative tools compress briefing, moodboarding, concept variants, image manipulation, layout iteration and production adaptation. BLS describes the core work as creating visual concepts, layouts, logos and final production using software, with client revisions and error review [S2]. The surviving work is more likely to be client interpretation, choice among alternatives, brand-system stewardship, approval management and accountability; it is not a reason to assume the original production hours remain billable.
narrative.value_capture: There is a narrow service layer around trusted client relationships, proprietary brand systems and senior art direction, but it is weakly portable in the small-firm population. Upwork's marketplace evidence shows worldwide graphic-design rates around $15–$35 per hour and frequent fixed-price work, indicating transparent comparison and price pressure rather than a protected, recurring asset base [S3]. A portfolio and client trust can matter, but neither is a durable code-wide moat when customers can generate a first draft or source freelancers globally.
narrative.adoption_timing: I estimate effective adoption has already crossed 50%, so t50 is set to zero under the frozen rule. That is an inference, not a code-specific census: Adobe surveyed nearly 2,000 creatives, reports 38% daily use overall, 40.9% daily use among graphic designers, and a large pragmatic group that is mostly weekly users [S1]. Adoption is uneven by task and buyer, but the commercial workflow has already crossed the screen's 50% timing boundary; the remaining uncertainty concerns depth of substitution and price pass-through, not first use.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The supplied band contains 370 firms but the underlying firm distribution is overwhelmingly below five employees. That makes founder/key-person dependence, customer concentration and individual portfolios central diligence risks. The available independent-creative-agency transaction report is a broad, upper-market comparator, not a 541430 lower-middle-market comp; it says traditional service models command lower values than technology-driven agencies and describes selective strategic acquisitions [S4]. I therefore use one minimally confirmed strategic consolidator and set buy/exit assumptions conservatively rather than importing the report's broad-sector headline multiples.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is MELTING. Clients will still need decisions, brand governance, high-stakes launches and accountable creative leadership, but the code's core deliverable—layouts, logos, graphics and production assets—is increasingly obtainable through software, templates or global freelancers. BLS's projection explicitly notes that automated design tools such as AI may reduce companies' need to contract with freelance graphic designers [S2]. This is product replacement risk, not merely a temporary labor-efficiency cycle.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The score is intentionally fragile. The immutable role mix is at 4-digit 5414 and its largest listed occupation, Interior Designers, is identified in the brief as sibling-code contamination; I do not reweight it. Adobe is a multi-country survey of creatives rather than a NAICS revenue study, marketplace rates are not audited firm economics, and the M&A report covers independent creative agencies rather than small pure-play graphic-design firms [S1][S3][S4]. These limitations lower confidence and favor a kill rather than a precision valuation claim.

sources.title: Adobe, How Creatives are Thinking About AI
sources.what_it_supports: June 2026 survey of nearly 2,000 creatives: 38% daily AI use overall; graphic designers lead the listed roles with 40.9% daily use; the largest segment is mostly weekly users. Used for adoption timing and workflow exposure, not NAICS revenue share.

sources.title: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Graphic Designers Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.what_it_supports: Defines graphic-design tasks such as layouts, logos, concepts, client revisions and final production; reports 2% projected employment growth for 2024–34 and notes AI may reduce companies' need to contract with freelance graphic designers.

sources.title: Upwork, Graphic Designer Hourly Rates
sources.what_it_supports: Marketplace indication of $15–$35 median hourly rates, a wider $15–$150 range, and common fixed-price engagements. Used only to characterize transparent, project-based pricing and global freelance alternatives.

sources.title: J&A Sell-Side M&A Report, Independent Creative Agencies M&A Transactions and Valuations
sources.what_it_supports: Broad independent-creative-agency M&A context through Q4 2024, including the distinction between technology-driven agencies and traditional service models and examples of strategic acquisitions. It is not a pure-play 541430 small-firm valuation comp.

sources.title: Gallup, Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.what_it_supports: Fall-2024 survey of U.S. business owners showing nonemployer businesses are less likely to expect a sale or transfer and more likely to expect closure or uncertainty. Used only as generic small-owner succession context.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Weighted exposure estimate based on software-centered graphic-design tasks and current creative-tool use. It preserves every immutable role share, discounts the contaminated Interior Designers row, and assigns a high but uncertain fraction to the unlisted residual because it likely contains junior production and adjacent design labor.
caveats: Role weights are supplied at 4-digit 5414, not 541430.
The 27-1025 Interior Designers row is known sibling-code contamination and is not treated as representative of the target code.
No task-frequency survey maps automation shares directly to these wage shares.

role_judgments.rationale: Sibling-contaminated interior-design row; only limited visual-concept and rendering work is analogous to the target code, so this row is heavily discounted.
BLS identifies software-based layouts, logos, visual concepts, revisions and production as central duties; Adobe shows graphic designers lead listed creative roles in daily AI use [S1][S2].
Scheduling, proposals and reporting compress, while client ownership, hiring and commercial judgment remain human-accountable.
Prospecting material, qualification and CRM work automate partly; relationship selling and project scoping remain less substitutable.
Brief summaries, schedules, status reporting and revision routing are tool-friendly, although client escalation remains human.
General office coordination and document handling are highly automatable relative to creative-client work.
Physical moving and handling have little direct generative-AI substitution.
Research synthesis, basic audience analysis and first-draft marketing material are substantially compressible, while strategy and accountability remain.
Calendar, correspondence, intake and records work are highly automatable.
AI can accelerate concept generation and versioning, but senior visual judgment, approval and client leadership retain meaningful human work.
The material unlisted wage residual is uncertain; in this code it is conservatively treated as predominantly production-adjacent and junior design labor rather than as hidden protected IP work.

pi_dist.rationale: Portfolio reputation and local client relationships create some differentiation, but BLS describes widely accessible software-based work and Upwork shows comparable global project supply.
caveats: No code-level buyer survey measures differentiation.

pi_moat.rationale: Brand standards, client context and accountable senior review can protect a small share, but the principal output remains software-mediated visual production with increasing AI enablement.
caveats: This is a service moat judgment, not a claim of transferable proprietary technology.

t50_years.rationale: Daily use is 40.9% among graphic designers and Adobe reports a large mostly-weekly pragmatic cohort, supporting the estimate that effective workflow adoption has crossed 50% already.
t50_years.plausible_range: 0 now; if adoption is materially below 50%, 1–3 years
caveats: Adobe's sample is creative professionals and other creatives across three countries, not a U.S. NAICS establishment census.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Estimated effective adoption slightly above 50% from 40.9% reported daily use among graphic designers plus non-daily users in the mostly-weekly pragmatic cohort.
caveats: The source does not publish a 541430-specific all-frequency adoption rate.

historical_analogs.value: Desktop publishing, templates and digital production software reduced the scarcity of routine layout work; generative AI extends that pattern to ideation and asset creation, while client review and senior accountability persist.
historical_analogs.rationale: BLS documents software-centric production and the AI contracting risk; the historical comparison is an analytical analogy rather than a measured causal estimate.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Routine production compresses more than senior client-facing work
caveats: No code-specific historical price series was located.

selection.rationale: A deliberately low-confidence 20% assumption for the target code; Gallup supplies only generic context that many small and nonemployer businesses lack transfer plans, not owner age by NAICS.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Generic small-business uncertainty is documented, but no evidence establishes a 541430-specific shortage of sellable firms or succession supply-demand imbalance.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false unless code-specific evidence is found
caveats: Do not convert generic closure and uncertainty data into a code-specific acquisition pipeline.

active_consolidators.rationale: One minimally confirmed strategic-acquirer case is counted from the broad creative-agency report; the available evidence does not demonstrate a deep, current 541430 lower-middle-market roll-up field.
caveats: The report's transaction set extends beyond pure graphic design and includes larger agencies.

buy_mult.rationale: A conservative illustrative entry multiple for a small, key-person-dependent service firm. The broad source says traditional service models command lower values than technology-driven agencies, but it cannot provide a code-specific LMM price.
caveats: Not a transaction-comp-derived 541430 multiple; no investment recommendation follows.

exit_mult.rationale: Illustrative exit multiple below entry reflects AI-driven product replacement risk, project pricing and limited portable moat; broad creative-agency M&A evidence does not offset those code-level risks.
caveats: This is an underwriting stress assumption, not an observed valuation statistic.

pricing_structure.value: Hourly and fixed-project fees, with rush premiums and scope/revision sensitivity
pricing_structure.rationale: Upwork describes hourly rates and common fixed pricing for graphic-design work, including scope, expertise and timeline effects.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Project and hourly pricing; retainers may occur but are not established as dominant
caveats: Marketplace historical contracts are not a census of U.S. agency billing.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. The role-weighted exposure estimate is high and current effective adoption is estimated above 50%; the A=10 result reflects the frozen t50 boundary, not a claim that all work or revenue disappears immediately.

terminal_value.justification: The central deliverable is replicable visual-production output. Senior brand governance and client accountability survive, but they are too narrow and key-person-dependent to establish durable code-wide terminal demand.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No invented fallback was used for immutable dataset fields. The supplied 4-digit 5414 role mix is itself a disclosed proxy for 541430 and includes known 541410 sibling contamination; it is preserved rather than reweighted.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Hourly and fixed-project pricing exposes production hours to automation, template substitution and worldwide freelance comparison. Faster generation can lower revenue per project even when a human remains responsible for the approved output.

dataset_mismatch.description: Role mix is at 4-digit 5414 rather than 541430, and the prompt identifies its 27-1025 Interior Designers wage share as sibling 541410 contamination.
sensitivity: The contaminated row is assigned only 0.25 automation rather than used as a proxy for pure graphic design.
The residual share is large (37.39%), making the V estimate materially uncertain.
No immutable data are reweighted or replaced.

subsegments: Standalone graphic design and brand-asset production
Web design/programming in 541511/541512 excluded except graphic-only web work
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Standalone visual design, layout, logo and brand-asset production
market_boundary.selection_basis: Frozen research brief and BLS description of graphic-design duties; the screen does not treat broader advertising, consulting, software or printing economics as target-code evidence.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0 now; if effective adoption is below 50%, 1–3 years
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A is 10 at zero and falls to 10/t50 if the threshold has not yet been crossed.
The contaminated role mix and large residual could move V materially, although V is already capped at 10 under the selected estimate.
Exit at or below entry produces M=0; evidence is not adequate for a positive-arbitrage underwriting claim.

reviewer_note: Fresh live sources were reopened on 2026-07-21. This is a kill-screen under explicit product-replacement risk, not a claim that all graphic-design demand ends. Treat the broad creative-agency M&A evidence and generic succession evidence as contextual only.
